entities.name: AKAP5
mentioned_activities: anchors PKA and CaN to GluA1

interactions.quote: If PKA is not able to phosphorylate GluA1 S845, CP-AMPAR recruitment to synapses during LTD is blocked
interactions.confidence_note: Kinase-substrate relationship is a standard biochemical activity.

interactions.quote: GluA1 undergoes dephosphorylation of T840 and S845 during LTD by protein phosphatases PP1/PP2A and CaN, respectively
interactions.confidence_note: Phosphatase-substrate relationship is a standard biochemical activity.

interactions.quote: AKAP5 anchors both PKA and CaN to GluA1 allowing for the efficient coordination of these signaling pathways during LTD
interactions.confidence_note: The protein acts as a scaffold/anchor, not an enzyme.

interactions.quote: A phosphorylation site was identified in the first intracellular loop of GluA1 (S567) which is phosphorylated by CaMKII and promotes removal of GluA1 from synapses
interactions.confidence_note: Kinase-substrate relationship is a standard biochemical activity.

interactions.quote: Scaling-up appears to involve increased tyrosine phosphorylation of GluA2 as a result of downregulation of the tyrosine phosphatase, STEP61

gaps: Only binding described for AKAP5 to PKA/CaN — molecular function (if any beyond scaffolding) unknown.
Only binding described for GRIP1/2 to GluA2 — molecular function unknown.
The mechanism of how CaN 'activation' leads to GluA1 removal is not clearly defined in the text.
questions_for_expert: Is 'anchoring' by AKAP5 considered a 'protein binding' molecular function, or should this be modeled exclusively as a complex assembly/location relation?
Does the text imply that CaN is an enzyme or a recruiter in the context of CP-AMPAR removal?
Should we model 'lysosomal degradation of PICK1' as a molecular process in the GO-CAM or is this an outcome of a regulatory chain?
